examples.title: Normalise a distribution
examples.description: Tests whether the learner can divide each element of a list by the total sum to produce a valid probability distribution. This mirrors what pymdp's norm_dist does under the hood.
examples.template:
def normalise(values):
    total = sum(values)
    # ___BLANK___  (return a new list where each element v is divided by total)
    pass

tests.code:
result = normalise([3, 1])
assert abs(sum(result) - 1.0) < 0.001, f'Expected sum 1.0, got {sum(result)}'

result = normalise([3, 1])
assert abs(result[0] - 0.75) < 0.001, f'Expected result[0] to be 0.75, got {result[0]}'
tests.description: First element is correct for input [3, 1]

tests.code:
result = normalise([3, 1])
assert len(result) == 2, f'Expected length 2, got {len(result)}'
tests.description: Result has the same length as the input

examples.title: Check if a distribution is valid
examples.description: Tests whether the learner understands the two rules that make a list of numbers a valid probability distribution: all values must be non-negative and they must sum to 1.0.
examples.template:
def is_valid_distribution(values):
    all_non_negative = all(v >= 0 for v in values)
    total = sum(values)
    # ___BLANK___  (return True if all_non_negative is True AND total is within 0.001 of 1.0)
    pass
examples.solution:
def is_valid_distribution(values):
    all_non_negative = all(v >= 0 for v in values)
    total = sum(values)
    return all_non_negative and abs(total - 1.0) < 0.001

tests.description: Returns True for a valid distribution [0.3, 0.5, 0.2]

tests.code: assert is_valid_distribution([1, 2, 3]) == False, 'Expected False for [1, 2, 3] (sums to 6, not 1)'
tests.description: Returns False when values do not sum to 1

tests.code: assert is_valid_distribution([-0.1, 0.6, 0.5]) == False, 'Expected False for [-0.1, 0.6, 0.5] (negative value)'
tests.description: Returns False when a value is negative
